2604.24170.title: Credal Concept Bottleneck Models for Epistemic-Aleatoric Uncertainty Decomposition
2604.24170.paper_summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) predict through human-interpretable concepts, but they typically output point concept probabilities that conflate epistemic uncertainty (reducible model underspecification) with aleatoric uncertainty (irreducible input ambiguity). This makes concept-level uncertainty hard to interpret and, more importantly, hard to act upon. We introduce CREDENCE (Credal Ensemble Concept Estimation), a CBM framework that decomposes concept uncertainty by construction. CREDENCE represents each concept as a credal prediction (a probability interval), derives epistemic uncertainty from disagreement across diverse concept heads, and estimates aleatoric uncertainty via a dedicated ambiguity output trained to match annotator disagreement when available. The resulting signals support prescriptive decisions: automate low-uncertainty cases, prioritize data collection for high-epistemic cases, route high-aleatoric cases to human review, and abstain when both are high. Across several tasks, we show that epistemic uncertainty is positively associated with prediction errors, whereas aleatoric uncertainty closely tracks annotator disagreement, providing guidance beyond error correlation. Our implementation is available at the following link: https://github.com/Tankiit/Credal_Sets/tree/ensemble-credal-cbm
2604.24170.paper_summary_zh: 概念瓶頸模型（CBMs）透過人類可解釋的概念進行預測，但它們通常輸出將認知不確定性（可減少的模型不明確性）與隨機不確定性（不可減少的輸入模糊性）混合在一起的點概念概率。這使得概念層級的不確定性難以解釋，更重要的是，難以採取行動。我們介紹了 CREDENCE（Credal Ensemble Concept Estimation），這是一個通過構建來分解概念不確定性的 CBM 框架。CREDENCE 將每個概念表示為一個信念預測（概率區間），從不同概念頭之間的分歧中推導出認知不確定性，並通過專門的模糊輸出來估計隨機不確定性，該輸出經過訓練以匹配可用時的標註者分歧。生成的信號支持指導性決策：自動化低不確定性案例，優先收集高認知案例的數據，將高隨機案例轉交給人工審查，並在兩者都高時選擇不採取行動。在幾個任務中，我們顯示認知不確定性與預測錯誤呈正相關，而隨機不確定性則與標註者分歧密切相關，提供超越錯誤相關性的指導。我們的實現可在以下鏈接獲得：https://github.com/Tankiit/Credal_Sets/tree/ensemble-credal-cbm
2604.24170.author: Tanmoy Mukherjee et.al.
2604.24170.authors: Tanmoy Mukherjee, Thomas Bailleux, Pierre Marquis, Zied Bouraoui
